text: Hello friends, today we are going to present a laparoscopic right hemicolectomy for carcinoma cecum. This is a case of a forty-two year old female referred to us for further investigation for severe anemia. She had received injectable iron recently. We subjected her for upper GI endoscopy and colonoscopy. Upper GI endoscopy revealed normal. Coronoscopy revealed ulcerative proliferative growth at IC junction. It is just proximal to IC junction and this is the ulcerative proliferative growth. You can see here the narrow band imaging picture and then we have taken multiple biopsies from this particular growth. and send for histopathological reports.

Colonic biopsy revealed well-differentiated adenocarcinoma. The CT scan abdomen for this patient revealed focal thickening of cecum and ascending colon for a length of six point seven centimeters and it is fourteen mm in maximum thickness and there is an irregularity of underlying serosa and no significant luminal narrowing is detected. but there is no appendix is visualized on CT scan abdomen. There was no comorbidity, hemodynamically patient was stable, her hemoglobin was ten grams of gram percent and then she posted for laparoscopic right hemicolectomy. The team who managed this case was Dr. Rahul Mahadhar myself, surgeon and endoscopist. Dr. A.M. Arora was assisting surgeon. Dr. Dhanashree Mahadhar was a camera surgeon. Dr. Mrs. Sonia Naik was anesthetist. And Dr. Nirmal Thakur was physician to manage this case. This was the operation theatre setup. Surgeon is standing on the left side of the patient. Camera holder is standing in between the legs of the patient. Leg split position is used and reverse tender bug position is used most of the times for this particular operation. T-tube monitors are on two sides of the patient at shoulder level. and the scrub nurse at the right leg side of the patient.

The laparoscopic port setup for the right hemicolectomy includes a 10mm port at the suprapubic region, a 12mm port on the left side at the umbilicus level near the linea semilunaris, used for stapling, a 5mm port in the left subcostal region at the midclavicular line, a mirrored 5mm port on the right side in the right subcostal region at the midclavicular line, and a 5mm port in the right iliac fossa.

The surgeon lifts the right mesocolon and performs a bottoms-up dissection between the mesocolon and Gerota's fascia. Facial dissection is carried out, and vessels are taken before supracolic dissection around the hepatic flexure. The lateral attachments of the ascending colon and cecum are mobilized, followed by ileocolic side-to-side anastomosis. A cystoscopic lighted ureteral stent is inserted on the right side. The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg with leg split, and small bowel loops are shifted to the left. The ileocolic vessel pedicle is lifted, and a plane is created between the ileocolic vessels and mesocolon using a harmonic scalpel, visualizing the ileocolic vein amidst minor bleeding.

The surgeon identifies the origin of the ileocolic vessel and applies hemoclips and a ligar clip to secure it. Using a ligature, the vessel is cut, and dissection proceeds between the vessel and artery, addressing lymphatic tissue. The incision on the mesentery is extended to create a large avascular plane between the colon mesentery and posterior fascia. The duodenum is visible, and fascia over it is managed with a ligature. The right ureter with a lighted stent is seen, with fascia over it and colon mesentery above. The ileocolic vessel is clipped at its origin from the aorta.

The surgeon applies a lega clip and a hemo clip to the vessel, then cuts the vessel between the clips. Dissection is carried out laterally, revealing the duodenum covered by a thin, papery peritoneal layer. A hole is made in the peritoneum, allowing further dissection along the side of the opening.

The surgeon continues the dissection cranially and laterally, carefully navigating the facial plane near the ureter and duodenum. The dissection progresses between the Gerota's fascia and the mesentery, with meticulous attention to small vessels to prevent bleeding and potential organ injury.

The surgeon uses a ligature to dissect the serotus fascia and the facial plane between the duodenum and the mesentery, reaching the upper border of the duodenum. The dissection is carried out further laterally under visualization.
The surgeon dissects the embryological plane between the Gerota's fascia and the right side mesentery, carefully taking down all fissures within this plane.

The surgeon continues dissection laterally towards the pancreatic head, identifying and managing small vessels, including the right branch of the middle colic vessel, using a ligature. Dense adhesions between the pancreatic head and mesentery are carefully cut to prevent bleeding. The gallbladder is visible from below, and dissection progresses around the bowel, addressing remaining attachments in the supracolic compartment.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the hepatic flexure of the colon, mobilizing it entirely. Adhesions between the gallbladder and mesentery are addressed using a ligature. Dissection begins in the supracolic compartment, with adhesions to the liver bed being carefully separated. The lateral attachments of the cecum are cut using a harmonic scalpel, fully mobilizing the right side colon. The glistening structure of the right ureter is visible, with a lightning catheter present, as the lateral attachments are meticulously cut with the harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon visualizes the kidney, ureter, and duodenum while addressing the terminal ileum mesentery using a ligature. The mesentery is dissected, reaching the bowel wall, and fatty tissue around the cut portion is managed. The transverse colon is prepared, and small mesenteric attachments are dissected using a ligature. ICG mapping is performed to prepare the site for resection.

The surgeon completes the resection site preparation for the transverse colon. ICG dye is administered, revealing the vascularity of the colon with a clear line of demarcation indicating the transection level. The transverse colon is transected using a 60 mm purple endo-GIA stapler, showing a difference in vascularity between the two ends. The small bowel is also transected, with visible congestion at the cut portion and normal appearance in the rest. The ICG mapping shows vascularity on the left side and lack of vascularity on the right side, guiding the transection level for the small bowel.

The specimen is positioned on the right side of the patient. The small bowel is mobilized to facilitate a side-to-side ileocolic anastomosis. Stay sutures are placed at the transected ends of the ileum and transverse colon. An Endo GIA 60 mm purple stapler is used to perform the anastomosis. An enterotomy is created on both the small bowel and transverse colon sides using a hook. The stapler is introduced, approximated, and fired. The staple line is inspected for hemostasis, confirming no bleeding. The enterotomy is closed with a U-Zero V-Lock barbed suture, completing the first layer closure.

The surgeon completes a full-thickness closure of the anastomosis site with a second layer of sutures. The rent between the middle of the colon and the small bowel mesentery is closed using continuous sutures to prevent internal herniation. ICG mapping is performed to confirm that vascularity is well maintained on both sides of the anastomosis. A transverse suprapubic incision of approximately four centimeters is made, and a wound protector is introduced to deliver the specimen through this incision.

And then this wound is closed in layers. The total operation time for this particular operation was approximately eighty-eight minutes. And this is the growth at the cecum. And started orals on third postoperative day, postoperative period was uneventful, discharged on fourth postoperative day. The histopathology report turned out to be nineteen nodes dissected. all free of tumors and the histopathology will suggest you have well-differentiated adenocarcinoma with T-N-O-M-O.
